title: Trump’s threats – and U-turn – on Hormuz fees further undermine US credibility in the Gulf
gist: Trump declared the US 'Guardian' of the Strait of Hormuz and demanded a 20% cargo-value passage fee, then reversed within a day in favour of Gulf trade and investment deals. Chatham House argues the episode was unlawful and unrealistic, eroded US credibility in the Gulf, weakened Washington's stance against Iranian tolls, and leaves a negotiated settlement as the only viable path to restoring shipping.

themes.theme: Trump's fee threat and rapid U-turn

insights.claim: On 13 July 2026 Trump declared the US the 'Guardian' of the Strait of Hormuz and demanded a payment of 20 per cent of each passing vessel's cargo value to guarantee passage.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A 20% cargo levy is a step-change in transit cost risk for oil and commodity flows through the world's most important chokepoint; traders must price sudden, unilateral policy shocks even when they prove short-lived.
insights.quote: The US would ensure passage through the Strait, he announced, but would ‘as a matter of FAIRNESS’ demand a payment of 20 per cent of the value of the cargo carried by each passing vessel.
insights.locator: On 13 July President Donald Trump appointed

insights.claim: The proposed fee could reach up to $30 million per passage for a large oil tanker — roughly 15 times the tolls Iran had reportedly demanded.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Quantifies the tail risk to tanker economics; a charge of this magnitude would make Hormuz passage commercially unviable and force rerouting or shut-ins that spike oil prices.
insights.quote: This could be up to $30 million per passage for a large oil tanker – an exorbitant amount and 15 times the sum Iran has reportedly demanded in tolls.
insights.locator: This could be up to $30 million

insights.claim: Trump paired the fee announcement with re-imposition of the US blockade on Iranian vessels, prompting Iran to confirm it would again close the Strait and returning shipping to its pre-ceasefire status.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The reciprocal escalation collapses the 17 June ceasefire framework, reintroducing supply-disruption risk to Gulf oil and fertilizer exports.

insights.claim: Within a day, on 14 July, Trump reversed course, replacing the reimbursement fee with 'Trade and Investment Deals' that Gulf states would make into the United States.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Signals that Gulf capital commitments, not maritime tolls, are the mechanism Washington will use to extract value — relevant for watching sovereign investment flows into US assets.
insights.quote: with Trade and Investment Deals that the various Gulf States will be making into the United States
insights.locator: On 14 July, a new post by the president

insights.claim: The article characterizes the sub-24-hour reversal as 'governance by Truth Social' that exposes how capricious the president is and how little weight can be placed on statements by his senior diplomatic officials.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Policy unpredictability itself becomes a priced risk factor; counterparties and allies discount official US commitments, raising the risk premium on any US security guarantee.
insights.quote: Trump’s governance by Truth Social has undercut this position yet again.

themes.theme: International law on straits and tolls

insights.claim: International law permits no state, however powerful, to appoint itself 'guardian' of other states' navigation rights and demand payment for unsolicited protection.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Establishes that the US scheme lacked any legal basis, meaning enforcement was never credible and the threat's real cost was reputational rather than operational.
insights.quote: International law does not permit any country to appoint itself ‘guardian’ of other states’ rights and demand payment.

insights.claim: Where a strait used for international navigation crosses a coastal state's territorial sea, that state must grant unhindered passage to all flag states, leaving no legal room for either Iranian tolls or the White House's larger demand.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Grounds the case that neither party can lawfully monetize passage, so any durable settlement must be fee-limited and service-based rather than a toll.
insights.quote: There is therefore no room in international law for Iran’s attempts to extract tolls, or for a similar and even more exaggerated attempt by the White House.

insights.claim: The IMO — described as the actual guardian of maritime freedoms — immediately opposed Trump's announcement, reiterating that it opposes all mandatory fees for passage through international straits.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Multilateral institutional pushback constrains unilateral chokepoint monetization and signals that the rules-based framework, not bilateral coercion, will govern any resolution.
insights.locator: The president’s fees announcement was immediately opposed

themes.theme: MoU, negotiations and the Malacca model

insights.claim: In the 17 June Memorandum of Understanding, the US conceded that Iran would restore free navigation for only 60 days, after which Iran and Oman would define future administration of the Strait — language Iran read as US acquiescence to its control.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The concession embedded a ticking 60-day clock and a structural weakening of the US legal position — a scheduled flashpoint markets should track for renewed Hormuz risk.
insights.quote: the US surprisingly conceded that Iran would restore freedom of navigation without charge ‘for 60 days only’
insights.locator: In the MoU, the US surprisingly conceded

insights.claim: Experts backing the Oman-hosted talks proposed a 'Malacca Strait model' in which coastal states collect only proportionate pilotage and navigational-services fees, administered without discrimination by a joint Iranian-Omani body.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Points to the likely shape of any face-saving deal — modest service fees, not tolls — which would cap the cost added to Gulf shipping and oil flows.
insights.locator: experts supporting the negotiations proposed a compromise

insights.claim: Despite mediation from Qatar and legal drafting from Europe, Iran remained intransigent in the Oman talks and no agreement was reached.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Diplomatic stalemate keeps the Strait's status unresolved, sustaining an elevated risk premium on Gulf transit until talks resume.
insights.quote: However, Iran remained intransigent in the negotiations, and no agreement was reached.

insights.claim: Iran claims to have shifted the Strait's sea lanes northward into its territorial waters, but the original mid-strait routing was agreed with the IMO in 1966 and cannot be unilaterally changed.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The routing fight is the physical proxy for control; a return to the 1966 mid-strait lane is the obvious compromise and a signal that de-escalation is underway.

insights.claim: The US and Oman, with IMO involvement, moved the sea lane southward to hug the Omani coast, and Iran responded with occasional missile and drone attacks on shipping using the US/Omani route.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Active kinetic threats on a specific lane mean insurers and owners face route-dependent war-risk pricing, not a blanket Strait closure.
insights.locator: The US and Oman, with the involvement of the IMO

insights.claim: The fee episode weakened the US position against Iranian tolls, since Washington had itself demanded charges fifteen times larger.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: US moral leverage in the Gulf is degraded, making it harder to build a coalition to deny Iran toll rights and prolonging the standoff.
insights.quote: This will also weaken the US position in opposition to Iran’s demand for tolls. After all, the US president had demanded them fifteen-fold.
insights.locator: This will also weaken the US position

insights.claim: The article traces the underlying crisis to the US and Israeli attack on Iran in February 2026, framing the current standoff as a downstream consequence of that strike.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Identifies the structural root of Gulf tail risk; without addressing the broader US-Iran conflict, chokepoint disruptions are likely to recur.
insights.quote: this crisis was brought about by the US and Israeli attack on Iran in February in the first place.
insights.locator: And ultimately this crisis was brought about

insights.claim: China, which also depends on Hormuz maritime freedoms, could make a significant contribution by pressuring for their restoration, while Europe under UK leadership could help secure navigation once a deal is done.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Flags potential external circuit-breakers; a Chinese diplomatic intervention would be a meaningful de-escalation signal for oil and shipping markets.
insights.quote: Other countries such as China could make a significant contribution by finally applying pressure in favour of restoring the maritime freedoms on which they also depend.
